network.overall_strategy: Ten convergent pathways connect simple C1–C2 feedstocks (CO₂, HCN, CO, H₂, NH₃, H₂S) to threonine across three prebiotic environments. Hydrothermal vents provide CO₂ reduction to formate/formaldehyde on greigite/magnetite and iron-sulfur-catalysed keto acid synthesis. Surface pools host cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland 2013; Patel et al. 2015) generating formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and lactaldehyde, which undergo Strecker or Bucherer-Bergs reactions to threonine aminonitrile or hydantoin. Biochemical-stage hydrolysis, aldol condensation, and HCN oligomer decomposition complete the network. Five hub molecules (HCN, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, threonine aminonitrile) create a densely interconnected bipartite graph with multiple redundant routes.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs on Fe3S4 cubane surface sites analogous to [Fe4S4] clusters in modern CO dehydrogenase/acetyl-CoA synthase. H2 dissociates on the mineral surface, providing hydride equivalents that reduce CO2 to formate via a surface-bound formyl intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite's Fe4S4 cubane units are structural analogs of active centers in modern hydrogenase and ferredoxin (White, Bhartia & Russell, Earth Planet. Sci. Lett., 2015). CO2 reduction to formate on FeS/greigite surfaces at alkaline vent conditions is thermodynamically favourable (Shock & Canovas, Science, 1998). Lane & Martin (J. Mol. Evol., 2012) showed that Fe(Ni)S minerals in alkaline vent micropores could concentrate organics ~5000-fold.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction, subseafloor hydrothermal zone

reactions.mechanism: Formate undergoes further 2-electron reduction on magnetite surface assisted by Fe0 as strong reductant. The reaction proceeds via surface-bound formyl species to yield formaldehyde, which is released into hydrothermal fluid. Magnetite catalyses Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis of reduced C1 species.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite catalyses Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis producing formaldehyde, methanol, and higher aldehydes (reviewed in McCollom & Seewald, Chem. Rev., 2007). Fe0 acts as the terminal electron donor for formate reduction. This connects deep-vent CO2 fixation to aldehyde feedstocks needed for Strecker synthesis at the surface.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of C1-C2 aldehydes from HCN

conditions.other: UV irradiation (254 nm), shallow evaporitic pool, phosphate buffer ~pH 7

reactions.mechanism: UV light drives Cu(I)-catalysed photoredox cycling of HCN with H2S as reductant. Sequential Kiliani-Fischer reductive homologation converts HCN first to formaldehyde, then to glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde via interconversion of cyanohydrins and aldehydes. Each homologation step involves HCN addition to form a cyanohydrin, followed by photoreduction of the nitrile to an aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2013) demonstrated each step experimentally: UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S converts HCN to formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde through photoredox cycling. Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) placed this within the broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism producing amino acid and ribonucleotide precursors simultaneously.

reactions.name: Formose-type aldol condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline evaporitic pool, borate stabilises sugar intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalysed aldol self-condensation: the umpolung step generates a nucleophilic carbanion from one HCHO that attacks a second HCHO, yielding glycolaldehyde (HOCH2CHO). Borate minerals stabilise the product against retro-aldol and Cannizzaro side reactions.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction (Butlerow, 1861) converts formaldehyde to C2-C6 sugars under alkaline conditions. Borate minerals stabilise sugars including glycolaldehyde and ribose (Ricardo et al., Science, 2004). Glycolaldehyde is the crucial branch point between sugar and amino acid chemistry (Patel et al., Nature Chemistry, 2015).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline conditions; 2-aminothiazole may selectively sequester glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde from complex mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of formaldehyde-derived enolate to the carbonyl of glycolaldehyde produces glyceraldehyde. Borate complexation protects the vicinal diol of glyceraldehyde from degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is central to Sutherland's ribonucleotide synthesis and directly reacts with HCN and NH3 to form the threonine aminonitrile (Patel et al., Nature Chemistry, 2015). Islam, Bucar & Powner (Nature Chemistry, 2017) showed that 2-aminothiazole selectively sequesters glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde from complex mixtures.

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation

conditions.catalyst: none required (spontaneous in presence of HCN)
conditions.other: facile nucleophilic addition, prebiotically spontaneous

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide ion (CN⁻) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde in a nucleophilic addition, yielding 2-hydroxypropanenitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin / lactonitrile). The reaction is rapid and nearly quantitative at mildly basic pH.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2013) demonstrated that acetaldehyde reacts with HCN to form acetaldehyde cyanohydrin as the key intermediate en route to lactaldehyde. This is a textbook cyanohydrin formation that proceeds spontaneously in aqueous HCN.

reactions.name: UV photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (254 nm), H2S as sacrificial reductant, 19% yield after 2 hours

reactions.mechanism: UV excitation of Cu(I)-cyanide complex generates a photoexcited state that abstracts an electron from H2S. The resulting reducing equivalent is transferred to the nitrile group of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, reducing it through an imine intermediate to the corresponding aldehyde (lactaldehyde). Sulfur is oxidized to elemental sulfur or polysulfides.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2013) demonstrated that photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin yields lactaldehyde at 19% yield after 2 hours of UV irradiation with Cu(I) cyanide and H2S. This is the key step specific to the threonine Strecker pathway.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: pH 7-9, one-pot Strecker conditions

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with NH3 to form an imine (Schiff base) via nucleophilic addition-elimination. HCN then adds to the electrophilic carbon of the imine (aminonitrile formation). The product 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile (threonine aminonitrile) forms as a racemic mixture of threonine and allothreonine diastereomers (~1:1).
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2013) demonstrated this classic Strecker chemistry: lactaldehyde + NH3 + HCN yields 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile. Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) identified this aminonitrile as one of four amino acid precursors arising as 'inevitable by-products' of the cyanosulfidic protometabolism.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: pH 7-9, alternative entry into Strecker aminonitrile

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde reacts with NH3 to form the corresponding imine. HCN addition yields 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile (threonine aminonitrile) plus the 2-amino-2,3-dihydroxypropanenitrile (serine aminonitrile). The C3 glyceraldehyde route provides a parallel entry to the same threonine aminonitrile as the C3 lactaldehyde route.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) showed that glyceraldehyde + NH3 + HCN directly yields the threonine aminonitrile, making glyceraldehyde 'perhaps the single most important branch point connecting RNA and protein precursor chemistry'. This provides a second entry from the sugar branch of the cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: pH-dependent hydrolysis; proceeds through amide intermediate; produces racemic threonine + allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Two-step hydrolysis: the nitrile group of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile is first hydrated to form the corresponding amide (threonine amide), which is then hydrolysed to threonine. Both steps are accelerated under mildly acidic or basic conditions and by elevated temperature.
reactions.reasoning: Standard nitrile-to-acid hydrolysis completes the Strecker synthesis. This is the terminal step described by Ritson & Sutherland (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2013) and Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015). The hydrolysis is thermally promoted and occurs spontaneously in warm aqueous solution. Threonine instability (Truong et al., Icarus, 2019) constrains the maximum temperature for this step.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.catalyst: mineral surface or divalent metal ions
conditions.other: thermodynamically unfavourable; requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; produces both threonine and allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: The α-carbon of glycine is deprotonated to form a nucleophilic carbanion (facilitated by metal-ion coordination or Schiff-base activation). This attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde in an aldol addition, forming a new C–C bond and generating threonine/allothreonine. The retro-reaction (threonine aldolase cleavage) defines the structural relationship.
reactions.reasoning: The biological retro-aldol cleavage of threonine (catalysed by threonine aldolases) yields glycine + acetaldehyde (Fesko, Appl. Microbiol. Biotechnol., 2016). The reverse aldol condensation is thermodynamically unfavourable but conceptually defines the simplest route. Koga & Naraoka (ACS Earth Space Chem., 2022) demonstrated related ammonia-aldehyde chemistry producing hydroxy amino acids from labeled aldehydes.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of glycine from formaldehyde

conditions.other: one of the most facile Strecker reactions; glycine is the dominant amino acid in Miller-Urey experiments

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with NH3 to form methyleneimine (H2C=NH). HCN adds across the C=N bond to form aminoacetonitrile (H2NCH2CN), which hydrolyses to glycine. This is the simplest possible Strecker synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Glycine is the most abundant amino acid in Miller-Urey spark discharge experiments (Ring, Wolman, Friedmann & Miller, PNAS, 1972) and forms readily via the Strecker mechanism from formaldehyde + NH3 + HCN. This reaction feeds the aldol condensation pathway to threonine (rxn_011).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink; more stable than aminonitrile intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with HCN to form the cyanohydrin, which reacts with NH3 to form the aminonitrile. In the presence of CO2 (as carbonate/bicarbonate), the aminonitrile cyclises to form 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin via carbamoylation and intramolecular cyclisation. The hydantoin ring is a thermodynamic sink relative to the open-chain aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen & Krishnamurthy (Nature Chemistry, 2022) demonstrated the Bucherer-Bergs pathway for several keto acids to amino acids via hydantoin intermediates. Hydantoins have been detected in carbonaceous chondrites (Lerner & Cooper, Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta, 2005), consistent with this pathway operating in meteorite parent bodies. The hydantoin provides kinetic trapping advantages over direct Strecker.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to threonine

conditions.other: two-step ring opening: first to N-carbamoyl amino acid, then to free amino acid + CO2 + NH3

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalysed hydrolysis of the hydantoin ring proceeds in two steps: (1) ring opening at the less hindered C2–N3 bond to yield N-carbamoyl-threonine, then (2) decarbamoylation to release free threonine, CO2, and NH3. Elevated temperature accelerates both steps.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2022) demonstrated that hydantoins hydrolyse to free amino acids under mild prebiotic conditions. This pathway parallels extant biosynthetic routes and provides a robust alternative to direct aminonitrile hydrolysis. The hydantoin intermediate may persist longer than the aminonitrile, providing a slow-release reservoir for threonine.

reactions.name: CO-driven pyruvate synthesis on iron-sulfide surfaces

conditions.other: Wachtershauserian iron-sulfur world conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO binds to FeS surface sites and undergoes sequential carbonylation and reduction. Insertion of CO into surface-bound methyl (from prior CH4 or CO reduction) yields a surface-bound acetyl, which is carbonylated again and hydrated to form pyruvate. The mineral surface provides both the catalytic sites and the reducing equivalents from FeS oxidation to FeS2.
reactions.reasoning: Cody, Boctor & Hazen (Science, 2000) showed that carbonylated iron-sulfur compounds produce pyruvate from CO and FeS at hydrothermal conditions. Huber & Wachtershaeuser (Science, 2006) demonstrated CO-dependent formation of alpha-hydroxy and alpha-amino acids on Ni-Fe precipitates at 80-120°C.

reactions.name: Chain elongation of pyruvate to alpha-keto-beta-hydroxybutyrate

conditions.other: aldol-type chain elongation on mineral surface; formaldehyde adds to pyruvate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes aldol-type condensation with formaldehyde on the mineral surface. The enolate of pyruvate (stabilized by mineral surface coordination) attacks formaldehyde, producing 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate (alpha-keto-beta-hydroxybutyrate). The mineral surface provides Lewis acid activation of both substrates.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wachtershaeuser (Science, 2006) demonstrated that Ni-Fe precipitates catalyse C-C bond formation from simple precursors under hydrothermal conditions. Copley, Smith & Morowitz (PNAS, 2005) proposed that alpha-keto acids formed through chain-elongation reactions on mineral surfaces in a proto-metabolic network. The pyruvate-to-ketobutyrate step parallels biological threonine dehydratase (in reverse).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-keto-beta-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: pH 8, H2 as reductant; analogous to Muchowska et al. Fe(0)-promoted reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto group of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate undergoes nucleophilic addition of NH3 to form the corresponding imine (Schiff base). The imine is then reduced by H2 (activated on the mineral surface or by dissolved Fe2+) to yield threonine. The reaction is analogous to biological transamination but uses abiotic reductants.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma & Moran (Nature, 2019) demonstrated that Fe2+ promotes reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids at 70°C. Kaur et al. (Chem, 2024) extended this to H2-driven reductive amination over nickel catalysts at 22°C and pH 8. While threonine's specific keto acid was not explicitly tested, the principle is established for structurally analogous substrates.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and higher polymers

conditions.other: concentrated HCN solutions (>0.01M); can also proceed during eutectic freezing at -20°C

reactions.mechanism: HCN molecules undergo sequential nucleophilic additions: 2 HCN → aminomalononitrile; 4 HCN → diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN). Further polymerization yields dark heterogeneous HCN polymers incorporating both C-C and C-N bonds. Amino acid side chains are embedded within the polymer backbone.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo, Zorzano & Osuna-Esteban (Life, 2013) reviewed 24 experimental conditions and found that 12 of the 20 proteinogenic amino acids, including threonine, were detected in HCN polymer hydrolysates. Miyakawa et al. (Origins Life Evol. Biosph., 2002) froze NH4CN solutions for 25 years and obtained amino acids, showing cold environments drive HCN oligomerisation.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers to amino acids including threonine

conditions.other: prolonged hydrolysis (days to weeks); yields complex mixture with glycine dominant; threonine present at trace levels

reactions.mechanism: Acid or base-catalysed hydrolysis cleaves amide, nitrile, and imine bonds within the HCN polymer, releasing embedded amino acids including glycine (dominant), alanine, aspartate, and threonine. Yields depend on polymer structure and hydrolysis conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo, Zorzano & Osuna-Esteban (Life, 2013) confirmed threonine among 12 proteinogenic amino acids detected in HCN polymer hydrolysates across 24 experimental conditions. This provides a non-specific but robust route to threonine from the simplest nitrogen-containing prebiotic feedstock.

reactions.name: Ammonia-aldehyde synthesis of hydroxy amino acids

conditions.other: aldehyde + ammonia chemistry without HCN; produces families of C3-C4 hydroxy amino acids

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde react with ammonia through a series of aldol, Mannich-type, and reductive amination steps to produce C3 and C4 hydroxy amino acids including threonine, allothreonine, serine, and homoserine. The reaction network branches through multiple aldol/amino intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Koga & Naraoka (ACS Earth Space Chem., 2022) demonstrated that 13C-labeled aldehydes (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, glycolaldehyde) reacting with ammonia in aqueous solution produced families of hydroxy amino acids including C3 and C4 structural isomers matching distributions in CM and CR carbonaceous chondrites. This ammonia-aldehyde chemistry provides a route to threonine without requiring HCN.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: The primary experimentally validated route: HCN photoredox generates acetaldehyde, which forms a cyanohydrin with HCN, then is photoreduced to lactaldehyde. Strecker reaction with NH3 and HCN yields threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyses to threonine. Complete pathway demonstrated by Ritson & Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015).

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Alternative cyanosulfidic entry: HCN photoredox generates formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde, which undergo aldol condensation to glyceraldehyde. Strecker reaction of glyceraldehyde with NH3 and HCN yields threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyses to threonine. Connects RNA and amino acid precursor chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 to formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Deep-vent CO2 fixation on greigite produces formate, reduced to formaldehyde on magnetite. Formaldehyde undergoes formose-type condensation at the surface to glycolaldehyde, then glyceraldehyde. Strecker aminonitrile formation and hydrolysis yield threonine. Cross-environment pathway linking Hydrothermal and Surface.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 to formaldehyde to lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate and formaldehyde, transported to surface where cyanosulfidic chemistry converts it through acetaldehyde and cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde. Strecker reaction completes threonine synthesis. Full Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical chain.

pathways.name: Glycine + acetaldehyde aldol condensation
pathways.description: Glycine (from Strecker synthesis of formaldehyde) condenses with acetaldehyde (from cyanosulfidic photoredox) in an aldol reaction to form threonine directly. Thermodynamically unfavourable but represents the simplest structural disconnection. Reverse of biological threonine aldolase.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde (from cyanohydrin photoreduction) reacts with HCN, NH3, and CO2 to form 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin, which acts as a thermodynamic sink. Hydrolysis releases threonine. Hydantoin intermediates detected in carbonaceous chondrites.

pathways.name: Iron-sulfur world keto acid reductive amination
pathways.description: CO reacts with FeS to generate pyruvate under hydrothermal conditions. Chain elongation with formaldehyde produces alpha-keto-beta-hydroxybutyrate, which undergoes Fe2+-promoted reductive amination to threonine. Entirely hydrothermal pathway analogous to reverse citric acid cycle.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis
pathways.description: HCN polymerises under UV or eutectic freezing conditions to form DAMN and higher oligomers. Prolonged hydrolysis releases complex amino acid mixtures including threonine. Low-selectivity but robust pathway; threonine detected across 24 experimental conditions in HCN polymer hydrolysates.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 to keto acid to reductive amination
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formate then formaldehyde at hydrothermal vents. Formaldehyde feeds chain elongation of pyruvate (from CO + FeS) to alpha-keto-beta-hydroxybutyrate. Reductive amination with NH3 and H2 yields threonine. Fully hydrothermal pathway combining carbon fixation and amination.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino acid pathway
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde (from cyanosulfidic photoredox or formose chemistry) react with ammonia in aqueous solution to produce C4 hydroxy amino acids including threonine. HCN-independent route matching amino acid distributions in CM and CR carbonaceous chondrites.
